choices.why: GIPS mandates counting all portfolios managed by the firm, regardless of whether they’re handled internally or by third parties.

choices.text: Include all portfolios—discretionary, nondiscretionary, fee-paying, and non-fee-paying.
choices.why: This aligns with GIPS requirements, which demand comprehensive inclusion of all managed portfolios.

pills.title: Lower of Cost or Market (LCM) under US GAAP
pills.concept: Under US GAAP, inventory is valued at the lower of its historical cost or its market value. Market is defined as the replacement cost, but it’s capped by the net realizable value (selling price minus selling costs) and floored by net realizable value minus normal profit margin. If market is below cost, inventory is written down to market.
exam_tips: Always calculate the ceiling (NRV) and floor (NRV minus normal profit margin) when applying LCM.
Remember that IFRS uses net realizable value directly, without the floor adjustment.
pills.formula: Market = min(Replacement Cost, NRV, NRV - Normal Profit Margin)

question.stem: Kamp, Inc. has inventory with a historical cost of $43, replacement cost of $55, selling price of $50, and selling costs of $3. Normal profit margin is 10% of selling price. Using US GAAP LCM, what value should Kamp report on its balance sheet?

choices.why: This is the floor (NRV - normal profit margin), but LCM compares cost to market, not the floor directly.
Correct. Cost ($43) is lower than market (calculated as $47, the ceiling), so inventory is reported at cost.
This is the ceiling (NRV), but LCM takes the lower of cost or market, not the ceiling alone.

pills.title: Z-spread vs. OAS in Bond Valuation
pills.concept: The Z-spread is the spread added to each spot rate of a benchmark yield curve to match a bond’s price. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) adjusts the Z-spread by subtracting the value of embedded options, like call features, to reflect the bond’s true risk-adjusted yield. This distinction helps investors compare bonds with different features fairly.
exam_tips: Remember: Z-spread includes all risks, while OAS removes option risk.
Focus on how options impact yield spreads during calculations.
pills.formula: OAS = Z-spread − (value of call option in basis points)

question.stem: A callable bond has a Z-spread of 150 bps. If the call option is valued at 30 bps, what is the OAS?

choices.text: 120 bps
choices.why: Correctly subtracts the call option value from the Z-spread.

choices.text: 150 bps
choices.why: Forgets to account for the call option’s value.

choices.text: 180 bps
choices.why: Adds instead of subtracts the call option value.

pills.title: Forward Price and Net Cost of Carry
pills.concept: The forward price of an asset depends on its spot price, the risk-free rate, and the net cost of carry. Net cost of carry is the difference between the present value of benefits (like dividends) and costs (like storage) of holding the asset. A positive net cost of carry lowers the forward price because benefits outweigh costs.
exam_tips: Remember: Net cost of carry = PV(benefits) - PV(costs). If benefits > costs, this value is positive.
Use the formula F0(T) = [S0 - net cost of carry] × (1 + Rf)^T. Check signs carefully in calculations.

question.stem: Using a risk-free rate of 3%, calculate the no-arbitrage forward price for a 6-month contract with a spot price of $100 and a net cost of carry of $1.

choices.why: Correctly applies the formula: ($100 - $1) × 1.03^0.5 ≈ $101.52
Incorrectly uses annual compounding instead of semi-annual (1.03^1)
Wrongly subtracts net cost of carry twice or uses a negative sign

pills.title: t-Distribution in Confidence Intervals
pills.concept: When the population variance is unknown, we use the t-distribution to construct confidence intervals instead of the z-distribution. The t-distribution has fatter tails, leading to wider confidence intervals, especially with small sample sizes. The critical t-value depends on the sample size (degrees of freedom) and must be looked up in a t-table.
exam_tips: Always check if the population variance is known or unknown—this determines whether to use t or z.
Remember that for small samples (n < 30) with unknown variance, the t-distribution is required.

question.stem: A researcher calculates a 95% confidence interval for a population mean using a sample of 25 observations with an unknown variance. Which of the following is most likely correct?

choices.text: The t-distribution with 24 degrees of freedom was used.
choices.why: Correct. With n = 25, degrees of freedom = 24, and the t-distribution is required due to unknown variance.

choices.text: The z-distribution was used because the sample size is less than 30.
choices.why: Incorrect. The z-distribution is only appropriate if the population variance is known or the sample size is large (n ≥ 30).

choices.text: The standard error was calculated as s / √(n - 1).
choices.why: Incorrect. The standard error is s / √n, not s / √(n - 1).

pills.title: Fisher Effect
pills.concept: The Fisher effect explains how nominal interest rates are determined by real interest rates and expected inflation. When inflation rises, nominal rates increase to compensate lenders for losing purchasing power. This relationship is foundational for understanding how central banks adjust rates during inflationary periods.
exam_tips: Memorize the formula: nominal rate = real rate + expected inflation
Distinguish between real and nominal rates in questions about inflation adjustments

question.stem: According to the Fisher effect, which factor directly increases the nominal interest rate?

choices.text: Expected inflation
choices.why: This is correct. The Fisher effect explicitly links nominal rates to expected inflation.

choices.text: Real interest rate stability
choices.why: This is a distractor. While real rates are stable, they don't directly drive nominal rate changes.

choices.text: Central bank reserve requirements
choices.why: This is unrelated to the Fisher effect, which focuses on inflation and real rates.

pills.title: Broker-Dealer Conflict of Interest
pills.concept: Broker-dealers face a conflict of interest because they act as both brokers (seeking best prices for clients) and dealers (profiting from spreads). This dual role may lead them to prioritize their own profits over clients’ best interests, prompting traders to set limits on how orders are executed to mitigate bias.
exam_tips: Focus on the dual role of broker-dealers as both intermediaries and market participants.
Remember that traders often impose execution limits when dealing with broker-dealers to prevent conflicts.

question.stem: What is the primary conflict of interest faced by broker-dealers?

choices.text: They must balance client interests with their own profit motives from trading spreads.
choices.why: Correct. Broker-dealers act as both brokers (client-focused) and dealers (profit-driven), creating inherent tension.

choices.text: They must compete with exchanges for market share.
choices.why: Incorrect. Competition with exchanges is unrelated to the internal conflict of broker-dealers.

choices.text: They must manage multiple clients’ portfolios simultaneously.
choices.why: Incorrect. Portfolio management is a separate challenge, not the core conflict of broker-dealers.

pills.title: Put/Call Ratio as a Contrarian Indicator
pills.concept: The put/call ratio measures investor sentiment by comparing the volume of put options (bets on price declines) to call options (bets on price increases). A high ratio suggests more bearish sentiment, as investors are buying more puts relative to calls, while a low ratio indicates bullish sentiment. It’s considered a contrarian indicator—extreme values may signal market turning points.
exam_tips: Remember it's a contrarian indicator: high ratios suggest oversold markets, low suggest overbought.
Focus on the ratio's inverse relationship with market sentiment.
pills.formula: Put/Call Ratio = Put Volume / Call Volume

question.stem: A put/call ratio of 2.0 indicates which market condition?

choices.text: Strong bearish sentiment
choices.why: Correct: High ratios reflect more puts than calls, signaling pessimism.

choices.text: Strong bullish sentiment
choices.why: Incorrect: Low ratios (e.g., 0.5) indicate bullish sentiment, not high ratios.

choices.text: Market equilibrium
choices.why: Incorrect: A ratio of 1.0 would suggest balance, not 2.0.

pills.title: Roll Yield in Futures Contracts
pills.concept: Roll yield occurs when a futures position is closed and re-established in a new contract. It reflects the profit or loss from the difference between the spot price of the asset and the futures price at the time of rolling. For example, if a trader rolls a long oil futures contract and the spot price is higher than the new futures price, they gain from this price gap.
exam_tips: Roll yield is distinct from price return, which only measures changes in the futures price itself.
Remember that roll yield can be positive or negative depending on market conditions and the cost of carry.

question.stem: An investor holds a long futures position in copper and rolls it to a new contract. Which component of their return is directly tied to the gap between the current spot price of copper and the futures price of the new contract?

choices.text: Roll yield
choices.why: This is correct. Roll yield specifically measures returns from the difference between spot and futures prices during contract rollover.

choices.text: Price return
choices.why: This measures changes in the futures price itself, not the spot-futures gap.

choices.text: Collateral yield
choices.why: This term is unrelated to futures trading and refers to returns from margin deposits.

pills.title: Confidentiality Exceptions for Illegal Activities
pills.concept: CFA members must maintain client confidentiality, but this duty does not protect clients engaging in illegal activities. Sharing information about such activities to prevent harm is allowed, not a violation. The key is distinguishing between normal client privacy and illegal conduct.
exam_tips: Remember: Confidentiality does not apply to illegal activities or fraud.
Look for keywords like 'illegal,' 'criminal,' or 'fraud' in questions about confidentiality.

question.stem: A CFA charterholder learns a client is laundering money. They share this with authorities. Did they violate confidentiality standards?

choices.text: Yes, always maintain client confidentiality regardless of activity
choices.why: This ignores the exception for illegal activities, a common mistake.

choices.text: No, because the client’s actions are illegal
choices.why: Correct: Confidentiality exceptions apply to illegal activities, allowing disclosure to prevent harm.

choices.text: No, only if the client explicitly approves disclosure
choices.why: Confidentiality exceptions don’t require client approval for illegal activities.

pills.title: Calculating Beta Using Covariance and Correlation
pills.concept: Beta measures a security's sensitivity to market risk. It is calculated by multiplying the correlation between the security and the market by the ratio of the security's volatility to the market's volatility. This standardizes the covariance into a relative measure of risk.
exam_tips: Remember beta uses correlation, not just covariance
Always use the security's standard deviation divided by the market's

question.stem: A stock has a standard deviation of 30%, the market index has 20%, and their correlation is 0.8. What is the stock's beta?

choices.why: Correct calculation: 0.8 × (30%/20%)
Forgets to divide by market volatility
Incorrectly divides market volatility by security volatility

pills.title: LIFO vs FIFO in Falling Prices
pills.concept: When prices fall, LIFO assumes the most recent (cheaper) units are sold first, leading to lower COGS compared to FIFO. This results in higher gross profit under LIFO, which can increase tax payments. The difference between FIFO and LIFO COGS is tracked using the LIFO reserve.
exam_tips: Remember: Falling prices reverse the typical FIFO/LIFO COGS relationship.
Link LIFO reserve changes to COGS and gross profit adjustments.
pills.formula: FIFO COGS = LIFO COGS – (Ending LIFO Reserve – Beginning LIFO Reserve)

question.stem: If a company reports LIFO COGS of $500,000, with an ending LIFO reserve of $30,000 and a beginning LIFO reserve of $45,000, what is FIFO COGS?

choices.why: Correctly applies the formula: $500,000 – ($30,000 – $45,000) = $500,000 + $15,000 = $515,000. Wait, this answer is incorrect—see note.
Confuses the sign in the formula, subtracting $15,000 instead of adding.

pills.title: Credit Tranching in Securitizations
pills.concept: Credit tranching divides securitized assets into tranches with varying risk exposure. Subordinated tranches absorb losses first, protecting senior tranches. This creates a 'waterfall' structure where senior tranches receive payments only after subordinated tranches are repaid, offering higher credit ratings but lower yields.
exam_tips: Focus on how losses are absorbed sequentially in a senior/subordinated structure
Distinguish credit tranching from time tranching, which prioritizes principal repayments

question.stem: In a securitization with a $300M senior tranche, $80M subordinated A, and $30M subordinated B, which tranche absorbs losses first?

choices.text: Subordinated B absorbs losses first, then A
choices.why: Correct: First-loss tranches are structured to absorb initial losses

choices.text: Senior tranche absorbs losses first
choices.why: Incorrect: Senior tranches are protected by subordinated tranches

choices.text: All tranches absorb losses equally
choices.why: Incorrect: Credit tranching redistributes risk unevenly across tranches

pills.title: No-Arbitrage Pricing of Forward Rate Agreements (FRAs)
pills.concept: A forward rate agreement (FRA) locks in a future interest rate to hedge borrowing or lending risks. Its no-arbitrage price is calculated by creating a synthetic FRA using two loans: borrowing for a longer period and lending for a shorter period. This ensures the FRA rate matches the implied rate from the two loans, preventing risk-free profit opportunities.
exam_tips: Remember the synthetic FRA involves borrowing long and lending short to replicate the FRA's cash flows.
Focus on how interest rate correlations affect FRA pricing in different market conditions.
pills.formula: FRA rate = [(1 + L2*T2)/(1 + L1*T1)] - 1, where L1 and L2 are short-term and long-term rates, and T1/T2 are their time horizons.

question.stem: A company wants to calculate the no-arbitrage 30-day FRA rate for 90-day LIBOR using 30-day and 120-day LIBOR rates of 2.5% and 3.0%, respectively. What is the correct formula?

choices.why: Correctly applies the no-arbitrage formula using time-weighted rates for 30 and 120 days
Incorrectly uses a simple average instead of the time-weighted formula
Flips the order of rates, which would misprice the FRA

pills.title: Conflict of Interest in Consulting Work
pills.concept: CFA members must avoid actions that create conflicts between their professional duties and personal interests. If a CFA charterholder is asked to take on outside consulting work, they must first get written approval from their employer to ensure the new role doesn't compromise their existing responsibilities or create competing interests.
exam_tips: Always check if outside work requires employer consent
Conflicts arise when personal interests interfere with professional obligations

question.stem: Sally Albright, CFA, is asked by a business contact to take on consulting work analyzing distressed debt. She should:

choices.text: Accept if she gets consent from Frank & Company
choices.why: Candidates might think employer consent alone is sufficient, but the Standards require avoiding conflicts even with consent

choices.text: Reject the work as it creates a conflict of interest
choices.why: Correct answer. The Standards prohibit activities that create conflicts between personal interests and professional duties

choices.text: Accept if done on her own time with KDG's written consent
choices.why: Candidates may confuse 'own time' with resolving conflicts, but conflicts are about duty, not just timing

pills.title: Data Mining Bias in Research
pills.concept: Data mining bias occurs when researchers test many variables or patterns in data until they find statistically significant results, even if those results are just random noise. This overestimates the true significance of findings. To avoid it, studies should use out-of-sample data to test hypotheses, not the same data used to develop them.
exam_tips: Watch for studies that test numerous variables without disclosing most of them.
Remember that data mining bias is common in backtested trading strategies.

question.stem: Which of the following is a warning sign of data mining bias in research findings?

choices.text: The study tested hundreds of variables but only reported those with statistically significant results.
choices.why: This directly describes data mining bias, where significance is overstated by cherry-picking results.

choices.text: The research includes a strong economic theory to support the empirical findings.
choices.why: A solid economic theory is a positive sign, not a warning of bias.

choices.text: The study uses out-of-sample data to validate its trading strategy.
choices.why: Using out-of-sample data actually reduces data mining bias, making this a solution, not a warning sign.

pills.title: Monetary Transmission Mechanism
pills.concept: The monetary transmission mechanism explains how central bank actions (like changing interest rates) affect the economy. Key channels include: 1) higher rates increase borrowing costs, 2) lower asset prices reduce household wealth, 3) a stronger currency hurts exports, and 4) lower spending expectations slow growth. These effects collectively influence inflation and economic activity.
exam_tips: Focus on the four main channels: interest rates, asset prices, exchange rates, and expectations.
Contrary to intuition, contractionary policy often reduces inflation by lowering aggregate demand.

question.stem: Which of the following is a direct effect of a contractionary monetary policy?

choices.text: Decrease in aggregate demand
choices.why: Correct. Higher interest rates reduce borrowing and spending, directly lowering aggregate demand.

choices.text: Increase in government spending
choices.why: Incorrect. Government spending is a fiscal policy tool, not a direct effect of monetary policy.

choices.text: Rapid GDP growth
choices.why: Incorrect. Contractionary policy typically slows GDP growth by reducing economic activity.

pills.title: Capitalizing vs Expensing: Impact on Net Income
pills.concept: Capitalizing costs delays expense recognition, boosting net income in the first year compared to expensing. Over time, capitalized costs reduce net income as depreciation is recorded, while expensing lowers income immediately but leaves future earnings unaffected. Total net income over the asset’s life remains the same.
exam_tips: Focus on timing differences: capitalization smooths expenses, while expensing creates a one-time hit.
Remember that ratios like ROA and ROE are skewed initially by capitalization but normalize later.

question.stem: If a company capitalizes a $100,000 expenditure instead of expensing it, what is the immediate effect on net income?

choices.text: Net income increases by $100,000
choices.why: Capitalizing defers the expense, so income is higher in the initial period.

choices.text: Net income decreases by $100,000
choices.why: This would be true if the cost was expensed immediately, not capitalized.

choices.text: Net income remains unchanged
choices.why: This ignores the fundamental difference between capitalization and expensing.

pills.title: Leverage Ratio in Margin Transactions
pills.concept: The leverage ratio measures how much an investor borrows compared to their own funds. It is calculated by dividing the total value of the investment by the investor’s equity. Higher leverage amplifies both potential gains and losses.
exam_tips: Use total asset value divided by investor equity, not the other way around.
Leverage magnifies returns but increases risk from borrowed funds.
pills.formula: Leverage Ratio = Total Asset Value / Investor Equity

question.stem: An investor purchases 1,000 shares of a stock at $20 per share using a 50% initial margin. What is the leverage ratio?

choices.why: Miscalculates equity as 60% of the purchase price instead of 50%.
Confuses leverage ratio with a 33% margin requirement instead of 50%.

pills.title: GIPS Verification Requirements
pills.concept: GIPS compliance requires verification by an independent third party, not the firm’s internal team or regulators. The verification process must ensure that performance reports meet GIPS standards without conflicts of interest.
exam_tips: Remember: Only independent third parties can verify GIPS compliance.
Watch out for distractors suggesting internal departments or regulators as valid verifiers.

question.stem: An investment firm claims its GIPS compliance was verified by its internal compliance team and the SEC. Is this acceptable under GIPS?

choices.text: No, because only one party may verify GIPS compliance.
choices.why: This misrepresents the rule; the issue is the lack of independence, not the number of verifiers.

choices.text: No, because neither the internal team nor the SEC qualifies as an independent verifier.
choices.why: Correct. GIPS requires an independent third party, not internal staff or regulators.

choices.text: Yes, because the SEC’s involvement adds credibility.
choices.why: Incorrect. Regulatory approval does not satisfy GIPS verification requirements.

pills.title: Kondratieff Wave Cycle Length
pills.concept: The Kondratieff wave is a long-term economic cycle lasting about 54 years. Technical analysts use it as part of cycle theory to predict market trends by identifying patterns that repeat over this extended timeframe.
exam_tips: Remember key cycles: 4-year presidential, 10-year decennial, 18-year, and 54-year Kondratieff waves.
Distinguish Kondratieff from shorter cycles like the 18-year pattern.

question.stem: Which of the following is the cycle length for the Kondratieff wave?

choices.text: 18 years
choices.why: This refers to another cycle favored by technical analysts, not the Kondratieff wave.

choices.text: 36 years
choices.why: This is not a recognized cycle in technical analysis.

choices.text: 54 years
choices.why: Correct. The Kondratieff wave is specifically associated with a 54-year cycle.

pills.title: Commodity Futures Pricing
pills.concept: The price of a commodity futures contract depends on the current spot price, adjusted for the risk-free rate, storage costs, and the convenience yield. The formula captures how these factors influence future prices, with the convenience yield (benefit from holding the physical commodity) reducing the futures price.
exam_tips: Memorize the formula components and their roles in pricing.
Recognize that convenience yield is subtracted, not added.
pills.formula: Futures price ≈ Spot price × (1 + risk-free rate) + storage costs - convenience yield

question.stem: Which formula correctly estimates the price of a commodity futures contract?

choices.text: Futures price ≈ Spot price × (1 + risk-free rate) + storage costs - convenience yield
choices.why: Correctly applies the formula from the reference material.

choices.text: Futures price ≈ Spot price × (1 - risk-free rate) - storage costs + convenience yield
choices.why: Incorrectly inverts the risk-free rate and storage cost signs.

choices.text: Futures price ≈ Spot price + risk-free rate - storage costs - convenience yield
choices.why: Omits compounding and incorrectly subtracts storage costs.

pills.title: Conforming vs. Nonconforming Loans
pills.concept: Conforming loans meet strict standards set by agencies like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, such as maximum loan-to-value ratios and minimum down payments. Nonconforming loans fail these criteria and are typically securitized by private entities in nonagency RMBS. The distinction affects the risk and liquidity of mortgage-backed securities.
exam_tips: Remember that conforming loans are required for agency MBS, while nonconforming loans are excluded.
Link loan criteria (e.g., down payment, LTV) to the classification of conforming or nonconforming.

question.stem: Which of the following best defines a conforming loan?

choices.text: A loan that meets agency MBS eligibility criteria, including down payment and LTV requirements
choices.why: Correct. Conforming loans adhere to specific guidelines for inclusion in agency-backed securities.

choices.text: A loan securitized by private entities without agency guarantees
choices.why: Incorrect. This describes nonconforming loans, which are excluded from agency MBS pools.

choices.text: A loan with a balloon payment equal to the original principal
choices.why: Incorrect. Balloon payments relate to mortgage structure, not conformity standards.

pills.title: Swaps vs. Forward Contracts
pills.concept: An interest rate swap can be viewed as a series of forward rate agreements (FRAs), where each FRA has a contract rate equal to the swap’s fixed rate. However, unlike individual forward contracts (which are priced to have zero value at initiation), the FRAs in a swap may not be zero value because the swap’s fixed rate is determined to make the entire swap fair, not each FRA individually.
exam_tips: Focus on the analogy between swaps and multiple FRAs with the same fixed rate.
Remember that swaps differ from forward contracts in their value at initiation.

question.stem: How does a swap differ from a series of forward rate agreements (FRAs)?

choices.text: Swaps are standardized, while FRAs are customized.
choices.why: This confuses swaps with futures, not FRAs. Standardization is a futures feature, not a swap vs. FRA distinction.

choices.text: Swaps require daily margin adjustments, while FRAs do not.
choices.why: Daily margining applies to futures, not FRAs. This is a common confusion between forwards/futures and FRAs.

choices.text: A swap’s fixed rate makes the entire swap fair, but individual FRAs in the series may not be zero value at initiation.
choices.why: Correct. Swaps are structured so the overall contract is fair, but individual FRAs in the series may have non-zero values.

pills.title: Component Depreciation under IFRS
pills.concept: Under IFRS, firms must depreciate individual components of an asset separately based on their own useful lives. For example, a building’s roof and electrical systems are treated as distinct assets with different depreciation rates. This contrasts with U.S. GAAP, which permits but rarely uses this approach. Component depreciation provides a more accurate match between an asset’s cost and its consumption over time.
exam_tips: IFRS requires component depreciation, while GAAP allows but seldom uses it.
Always check if the question specifies whether components are treated separately.
pills.formula: Depreciation expense per component = (Component cost - salvage value) / component's useful life

question.stem: Global Airlines purchased a $50M airplane with a $3M interior expected to be replaced after 15 years. Using component depreciation, what is Year 1 depreciation expense?

choices.why: Correct: $47M aircraft (excluding interior) depreciates over 30 years ($1.4M) + $3M interior over 15 years ($200K)
Incorrect: This is the non-component method result, ignoring separate interior depreciation
Incorrect: Adds interior cost to total aircraft cost instead of subtracting it

pills.title: Calculating Beta Using Covariance
pills.concept: Beta measures how sensitive an asset's returns are to market movements. It is calculated by dividing the covariance of the asset's returns with the market's returns by the variance of the market's returns. This shows how much the asset's returns deviate with the market.
exam_tips: Always use variance (not standard deviation) of the market in the denominator.
Check units: covariance and variance must be in the same terms (e.g., decimal form).
pills.formula: β = Covariance of asset and market / Variance of market returns

question.stem: If the covariance of an asset's returns with the market is 0.048 and the market's variance is 0.04, what is the asset's beta?

choices.why: Mistakenly uses correlation coefficient (ρ) instead of covariance
Incorrectly divides covariance by standard deviation (0.2) instead of variance (0.04)

pills.title: Choosing Between t-Distribution and z-Distribution for Confidence Intervals
pills.concept: When the population variance is unknown, the t-distribution is used instead of the z-distribution for confidence intervals. This is because the t-distribution accounts for the additional uncertainty introduced by estimating the population standard deviation from the sample. The choice depends on whether the variance is known and the sample size.
exam_tips: Always check if the population variance is known or estimated from the sample.
For small samples (n < 30) with unknown variance, the t-distribution is essential to avoid underestimating confidence intervals.
pills.formula: Confidence interval = sample mean ± (t-value × standard error) when variance is unknown

question.stem: A researcher calculates a 95% confidence interval for a population mean with a sample size of 25. The population variance is unknown. Which statistic should be used?

choices.text: t-statistic
choices.why: Correct. The t-distribution is required when variance is unknown and sample size is small (n < 30).

choices.text: z-statistic
choices.why: Incorrect. The z-statistic assumes known population variance or a large sample size (n ≥ 30).

choices.text: Chi-square statistic
choices.why: Incorrect. The chi-square distribution is used for variance tests, not mean confidence intervals.

pills.title: Prohibition on Trading with Material Nonpublic Information
pills.concept: CFA charterholders must not trade using material nonpublic information, even if local laws permit it. The CFA Institute’s Standards override local regulations, and such trading violates Standard II(A), which forbids using confidential information for personal gain.
exam_tips: Remember that CFA Standards take precedence over local laws in ethical decisions.
Focus on whether the information is both material (significant to investors) and nonpublic.

question.stem: A CFA charterholder learns of a company’s upcoming merger before the public announcement. Local laws in the country do not prohibit trading on such information. What should the charterholder do?

choices.text: Avoid trading until the information is public
choices.why: Correct. Standard II(A) prohibits trading on material nonpublic information regardless of local laws.

choices.text: Trade immediately to capitalize on the opportunity
choices.why: Wrong. This violates Standard II(A) and risks disciplinary action, even if local laws allow it.

choices.text: Share the information with friends who are not CFA charterholders
choices.why: Wrong. Sharing nonpublic information also breaches Standard II(A) and could harm others.

pills.title: Neutral Interest Rate Formula
pills.concept: The neutral interest rate is the money supply growth rate that keeps economic growth stable. It equals the real trend growth rate (long-term sustainable growth) plus the inflation target. If the central bank's policy rate is above this neutral rate, policy is contractionary; below it, expansionary.
exam_tips: Remember the formula: neutral rate = real trend rate + inflation target
Watch out for confusion between real trend rate and inflation target in calculations
pills.formula: neutral interest rate = real trend rate of economic growth + inflation target

question.stem: A country's real trend growth rate is 2.5% and its inflation target is 3.0%. What is the neutral interest rate?

choices.why: Correct: 2.5% real growth + 3.0% inflation target equals 5.5%
Mistakenly uses only the inflation target
Incorrectly subtracts inflation target from real growth

pills.title: Stop-Sell Orders: Managing Market Momentum
pills.concept: A stop-sell order triggers a sale when a stock’s price drops to a specified level, helping investors lock in profits or limit losses. These orders are useful for selling overvalued stocks if prices decline, but execution prices may be unfavorable due to market volatility.
exam_tips: Remember that stop-sell orders execute when prices fall, not rise.
Distinguish stop orders from limit orders, which guarantee a price but not execution.

question.stem: An investor owns overvalued shares of a company and wants to sell them if the price drops significantly. Which order type should they use?

choices.text: Stop-buy order
choices.why: A stop-buy order triggers a purchase when the price rises, which is the opposite of the investor’s goal.

choices.text: Stop-sell order
choices.why: Correct. A stop-sell order executes when the price falls to a specified level, aligning with the investor’s need to sell during a decline.

choices.text: Limit order to sell at the current price
choices.why: A limit order guarantees a price but may not execute if the market moves against the investor.

pills.title: Revaluation Model under IFRS
pills.concept: Under IFRS, long-lived assets can be revalued to fair value if an active market exists. The revaluation model adjusts the asset’s balance sheet value to fair value at each revaluation date, with depreciation recorded between dates. Gains and losses from revaluation affect income or equity depending on prior revaluations.
exam_tips: First revaluations below carrying value create income statement losses, while gains increase equity (revaluation surplus).
Subsequent revaluations reverse prior income statement losses first before impacting equity.

question.stem: On its first revaluation, a company’s asset has a fair value $500,000 below its carrying value. How is this treated?

choices.text: Loss recognized on the income statement
choices.why: Correct: First revaluation losses directly reduce income.

choices.text: Decrease in revaluation surplus
choices.why: Incorrect: Revaluation surplus only applies to subsequent gains, not initial losses.

choices.text: No impact on income or equity
choices.why: Incorrect: The loss must be recognized in income under IFRS.

pills.title: GIPS Verification Requirements and Independence
pills.concept: GIPS verification requires an independent third party to confirm compliance with Global Investment Performance Standards. Unlike an SEC audit, which focuses on financial reporting accuracy, GIPS verification ensures performance standards are met. This process prevents conflicts of interest by requiring the verifier to have no prior involvement with the firm’s performance calculations.
exam_tips: Distinguish between GIPS verification and other types of audits (e.g., SEC audits).
Remember that independence of the verification firm is a non-negotiable requirement.

question.stem: Which statement about GIPS verification is correct?

choices.text: GIPS verification must be conducted by an independent third party not affiliated with the firm’s performance calculations.
choices.why: This correctly identifies the independence requirement, a core GIPS principle.

choices.text: An SEC audit can serve as valid GIPS verification.
choices.why: SEC audits focus on financial reporting, not performance standards, making them incompatible with GIPS verification.

choices.text: Firms may self-verify their compliance with GIPS standards.
choices.why: Self-verification violates the independence rule, which is central to GIPS integrity.

pills.title: Debt-to-Service Coverage Ratio (DSC) in CMBS
pills.concept: The Debt-to-Service Coverage Ratio (DSC) measures a commercial property's ability to generate cash flow to meet debt obligations. It is calculated by dividing Net Operating Income (NOI) by the required debt service payments. A higher DSC indicates stronger cash flow protection for lenders and investors, while a ratio below 1 suggests the property may not generate enough income to cover debt payments.
exam_tips: Remember: DSC is NOI divided by debt service, not the other way around.
A DSC below 1 is a critical warning sign of potential default.
pills.formula: DSC = Net Operating Income (NOI) / Debt Service

question.stem: Which formula correctly calculates the Debt-to-Service Coverage Ratio (DSC) for a commercial property?

choices.text: NOI divided by debt service payments
choices.why: This is the correct formula. DSC measures how much cash flow is available relative to debt obligations.

choices.text: Debt service payments divided by NOI
choices.why: This reverses the numerator and denominator, leading to an incorrect interpretation of coverage.

choices.text: NOI divided by loan-to-value ratio
choices.why: This mixes DSC with the loan-to-value ratio, which measures collateral value relative to the loan amount.

pills.title: Swap Replication with FRAs
pills.concept: An interest rate swap can be broken into multiple forward rate agreements (FRAs). For example, a one-year swap with quarterly payments is equivalent to four FRAs, each tied to a future payment date. Each FRA uses the swap's fixed rate as its contract rate, but unlike standard FRAs, these are 'off-market' because they don’t have zero value at initiation. The swap’s overall value remains zero at start because positive and negative FRA values offset.
exam_tips: Count the number of FRAs needed: a swap with quarterly payments requires four FRAs over one year.
Remember that FRAs in swaps use the swap’s fixed rate, not the floating rate.

question.stem: An investor wants to replicate a one-year fixed-for-floating swap with quarterly payments. Which statement is most accurate?

choices.text: The swap requires four FRAs, each tied to a 90-day LIBOR rate at the time of payment.
choices.why: Correct. Four quarterly payments mean four FRAs, each referencing the relevant 90-day LIBOR period.

choices.text: Each FRA in the swap uses the floating rate observed at initiation, not the swap’s fixed rate.
choices.why: Incorrect. FRAs in swaps use the swap’s fixed rate as the contract rate, not the floating rate.

choices.text: The total value of the FRAs at initiation is positive because floating rates are expected to rise.
choices.why: Incorrect. The swap (and its replicating FRAs) must have zero total value at initiation by design.

pills.title: One-Tailed vs. Two-Tailed Hypothesis Tests
pills.concept: A one-tailed test checks if a parameter is either greater than or less than a specific value, while a two-tailed test checks if it is simply different (either higher or lower). One-tailed tests use a single critical value, whereas two-tailed tests split the significance level between both tails of the distribution.
exam_tips: Look at the alternative hypothesis to determine test type: 'greater than' or 'less than' signals one-tailed; 'not equal to' indicates two-tailed.
Two-tailed tests are more common unless there is strong theoretical justification for a directional hypothesis.

question.stem: Which scenario requires a one-tailed hypothesis test?

choices.text: Testing whether a fund's return is greater than the market average
choices.why: This is a directional hypothesis (greater than), which requires a one-tailed test.

choices.text: Testing whether a fund's return differs from the market average
choices.why: This is a non-directional hypothesis (differs), requiring a two-tailed test.

choices.text: Testing whether a fund's return is exactly equal to the market average
choices.why: This would typically involve a two-tailed test, as it's about equality vs. inequality.

pills.title: Fiscal Multiplier Calculation
pills.concept: The fiscal multiplier measures how much aggregate demand increases from a change in government spending, considering taxes and the marginal propensity to consume (MPC). It is calculated as 1 divided by [1 minus (MPC multiplied by (1 minus the tax rate))]. Higher tax rates reduce the multiplier, while higher MPC increases it.
exam_tips: Always account for taxes in the denominator of the multiplier formula.
Remember that the multiplier effect is smaller when taxes are high because disposable income increases less.

question.stem: What is the fiscal multiplier if the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 80% and the tax rate is 25%?

choices.why: Correct calculation using the formula 1 / [1 – 0.8(1 – 0.25)] = 2.5
Mistakenly ignores taxes, using 1 / (1 – 0.8) = 5.0
Incorrectly applies tax rate directly: 1 / (1 – 0.25) = 1.33, rounded to 1.25

pills.title: Impact of Upward Revaluation on Net Income
pills.concept: Under IFRS, when a company revalues an asset upward, the gain is not recorded in net income but in other comprehensive income (OCI). This means the revaluation itself does not directly affect net income. However, future depreciation expenses increase because the asset’s book value is higher, which can lower net income in subsequent periods.
exam_tips: Remember that revaluation gains/losses under IFRS go to OCI, not net income.
Distinguish revaluation of PP&E from fair-value adjustments for trading securities, which do impact net income.

question.stem: A company revalues a building upward from $500,000 to $700,000. Under IFRS, what amount is recognized in net income this year?

choices.why: Revaluation gains under IFRS are recorded in OCI, not net income.
This assumes the gain is recognized in net income, which is incorrect under IFRS.
This might confuse depreciation adjustments with revaluation gains, but depreciation changes are not immediate.

pills.title: Effective Annual Rate (EAR)
pills.concept: The effective annual rate (EAR) shows the actual annual return earned on an investment, considering the effect of compounding. It adjusts the stated annual rate to reflect how often interest is compounded (e.g., monthly, quarterly). For example, 8% compounded quarterly gives a higher EAR than 8% compounded annually because interest is added more frequently.
exam_tips: Always use EAR when comparing investments with different compounding periods.
Remember the formula: EAR = (1 + r/n)^n - 1, where r is the stated rate and n is the number of compounding periods per year.

question.stem: What is the effective annual rate for a stated annual interest rate of 10% compounded quarterly?

choices.why: Incorrect. This assumes simple interest, ignoring compounding effects.
Incorrect. This might be a miscalculation of monthly compounding (10%/12)^12, but the question specifies quarterly.

pills.title: Support Levels
pills.concept: A support level is a price point where increased buying demand typically prevents further price declines. It acts as a floor for prices, often identified by previous lows or trendline breaks. When a stock’s price drops to this level and rebounds, it signals strong buyer interest.
exam_tips: Look for historical price lows or trendline intersections as potential support levels.
Resistance levels (opposite of support) act as ceilings, not floors.

question.stem: A stock’s price falls to $30, then rises to $31 after investors place buy orders. This scenario is most likely an example of:

choices.text: A support level
choices.why: Correct—buying demand at $30 halted the decline, indicating a support level.

choices.text: A resistance level
choices.why: Incorrect—resistance levels act as price ceilings, not floors.

choices.text: A change in polarity
choices.why: Incorrect—change in polarity refers to breached support becoming resistance, not initial support formation.

pills.title: Commodity Futures Pricing Formula
pills.concept: The price of a commodity futures contract is based on the current spot price, adjusted for the risk-free rate, storage costs, and the convenience yield. This relationship reflects the cost of carrying the commodity and the value of holding it for future use.
exam_tips: Remember that storage costs increase the futures price, while convenience yield decreases it.
This formula is an approximation and assumes no arbitrage opportunities.
pills.formula: Futures Price ≈ Spot Price × (1 + Risk-Free Rate + Storage Costs - Convenience Yield)

question.stem: Using the commodity futures pricing formula, which factor would most likely increase the futures price of a commodity?

choices.text: Higher convenience yield
choices.why: Convenience yield reduces the futures price, so this would lower it.

choices.text: Lower storage costs
choices.why: Storage costs directly add to the futures price; lower costs would decrease it.

choices.text: Higher risk-free rate
choices.why: A higher risk-free rate increases the cost of carrying the commodity, raising the futures price.

pills.title: CAPM Expected Return Calculation
pills.concept: The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) calculates a security's expected return based on its beta, the risk-free rate, and the market risk premium. The formula shows that expected return equals the risk-free rate plus beta multiplied by the market risk premium (market return minus risk-free rate).
exam_tips: Memorize the CAPM formula and understand each component's role.
Beta measures systematic risk, not total risk—common mistakes involve confusing the two.

question.stem: If the risk-free rate is 3%, the market return is 10%, and a stock has a beta of 1.5, what is its expected return according to CAPM?

choices.why: Incorrectly uses total risk instead of beta in the calculation.
Correct application of CAPM: 3% + 1.5*(10%−3%) = 13.5%.
Misapplies the formula by ignoring the risk-free rate entirely.

pills.title: Market Regulation Objectives
pills.concept: Market regulation aims to protect investors from fraud, ensure fair trading practices, and promote transparency. It also reduces information costs by standardizing financial reporting and ensures market participants maintain enough capital to meet obligations.
exam_tips: Focus on the four main goals: investor protection, fraud prevention, information transparency, and capital adequacy.
Distinguish between reducing information costs (a goal) and lowering accounting standards (not a goal).

question.stem: Which of the following is least likely to be an objective of market regulation?

choices.text: Reducing burdensome accounting standards
choices.why: This distracts by mentioning accounting standards, which are related but not a direct objective—regulation seeks to standardize, not reduce, them.

choices.text: Protecting unsophisticated investors
choices.why: This is a core objective to preserve market trust and prevent exploitation.

choices.text: Preventing insider trading
choices.why: This is explicitly mentioned as a key regulatory goal to maintain fair markets.

pills.title: Lockout Period in Credit Card ABS
pills.concept: Credit card ABS have a lockout period during which principal payments from cardholders are not distributed to investors. Instead, these payments are reinvested to buy new credit card receivables, maintaining the pool’s value. Investors only receive principal payments after the lockout ends, which can last 18 months to 10 years.
exam_tips: Remember: Lockout periods delay principal payments to investors, but interest is still paid during this time.
This feature is a common exam topic—focus on distinguishing lockout periods from other ABS structures.

question.stem: During the lockout period of a credit card ABS, which of the following is true?

choices.text: No new receivables are added to the pool.
choices.why: This is incorrect. Principal payments during the lockout period are used to purchase new receivables, keeping the pool’s value stable.

choices.text: Investors do not receive interest payments.
choices.why: This is incorrect. Interest payments are typically made periodically during the lockout period, though principal is not distributed.

choices.text: Investors do not receive principal payments.
choices.why: This is correct. Principal payments are reinvested during the lockout period and only distributed to investors after it ends.

pills.title: Swap Value at Initiation
pills.concept: A swap has zero value at the start because the present value of fixed-rate payments exactly matches the present value of expected floating-rate payments. If future rates rise, the fixed-rate payer gains value, while falling rates hurt them. The swap rate is set to balance these values, not to match current market rates.
exam_tips: Focus on present value equality, not just rate comparisons
Remember value changes with rate expectations, not contract terms

question.stem: At initiation, a swap has zero value because:

choices.text: The fixed rate equals the current market interest rate
choices.why: Candidates often confuse the swap rate with current rates, but it's set to balance future cash flows

choices.text: The present value of fixed payments equals the present value of expected floating payments
choices.why: This is correct - the swap rate is determined to make these values equal

choices.text: Both parties have equal credit risk
choices.why: Value depends on cash flow timing, not creditworthiness

pills.title: Effective Annual Rate (EAR) Calculation
pills.concept: The effective annual rate (EAR) shows the actual return on an investment after accounting for compounding. It is calculated by taking the periodic interest rate, raising it to the number of compounding periods per year, and subtracting 1. More frequent compounding increases the EAR compared to the stated annual rate.
exam_tips: Always divide the stated rate by the number of compounding periods to get the periodic rate.
Remember that EAR grows as compounding frequency increases, even if the stated rate remains the same.

question.stem: An investor is comparing two savings accounts. Account A offers 8% annual interest compounded monthly, while Account B offers 8% compounded semiannually. Which account has a higher effective annual rate (EAR)?

choices.text: Account A has a higher EAR because monthly compounding increases returns more than semiannual compounding.
choices.why: Correct. More frequent compounding leads to a higher EAR due to the power of compounding.

choices.text: Account B has a higher EAR because semiannual compounding avoids overcomplicating the calculation.
choices.why: Incorrect. EAR increases with more frequent compounding, not fewer.

choices.text: Both accounts have the same EAR because the stated rate is identical.
choices.why: Incorrect. The EAR depends on compounding frequency, not just the stated rate.

pills.title: IFRS Valuation Models for Property, Plant, and Equipment
pills.concept: Under IFRS, companies can choose between the cost model or the revaluation model for reporting property, plant, and equipment. The cost model uses historical cost minus accumulated depreciation, while the revaluation model adjusts assets to fair value. U.S. GAAP only allows the cost model and prohibits revaluation.
exam_tips: Memorize that IFRS permits revaluation, while U.S. GAAP does not.
Watch for distractors that mix IFRS and U.S. GAAP rules, like 'fair value model' (which is not an IFRS option).

question.stem: A firm using IFRS wants to report its factory building. Which valuation approach is most accurate?

choices.text: Cost model or fair value model
choices.why: Fair value model is not an IFRS option for PPE; IFRS only allows cost or revaluation.

choices.text: Cost model or revaluation model
choices.why: This is correct. IFRS explicitly allows these two models for PPE.

choices.text: Revaluation model or fair value model
choices.why: Fair value model is not permitted under IFRS for PPE; revaluation is allowed, but fair value is not.

pills.title: Type I and Type II Errors
pills.concept: A Type I error occurs when we reject a true null hypothesis, while a Type II error happens when we fail to reject a false null hypothesis. The significance level (alpha) determines the probability of making a Type I error, typically set at 5% or 10%.
exam_tips: Remember: Type I is a 'false positive' (rejecting a true null), Type II is a 'false negative' (failing to reject a false null).
Significance level (α) directly controls Type I error risk, but Type II depends on sample size and effect size.

question.stem: Which of the following best describes a Type I error in hypothesis testing?

choices.text: Rejecting a true null hypothesis
choices.why: Correct definition of a Type I error.

choices.text: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
choices.why: This describes a Type II error, not Type I.

choices.text: Accepting a false alternative hypothesis
choices.why: Confuses 'accepting' with 'failing to reject'; you never accept the null in hypothesis testing.

pills.title: Fiscal Policy Multipliers
pills.concept: Fiscal policy impacts aggregate demand through government spending and taxation. Government spending directly increases demand, while tax changes affect demand indirectly by altering disposable income. Spending multipliers are typically larger than tax multipliers because spending directly boosts economic activity, whereas tax cuts may be saved rather than spent.
exam_tips: Remember that direct government spending has a larger multiplier effect than tax cuts.
Consider the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) when calculating multiplier impacts.
pills.formula: Government spending multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC); Tax multiplier = -MPC / (1 - MPC)

question.stem: A government increases spending by $10 billion and raises taxes by $10 billion simultaneously. Assuming an MPC of 0.8, what is the net effect on aggregate demand?

choices.text: No change in aggregate demand
choices.why: This assumes equal impacts from spending and taxation, but spending multipliers are larger.

choices.text: Aggregate demand decreases by $2 billion
choices.why: This miscalculates the difference between spending and tax multipliers.

choices.text: Aggregate demand increases by $2 billion
choices.why: Correct: Spending multiplier (1/0.2 = 5) vs. tax multiplier (-0.8/0.2 = -4). Net effect = (5*10) + (-4*10) = +$10 billion.

pills.title: Present Value with Monthly Compounding
pills.concept: To find today's value of a future sum with monthly compounding, divide the annual rate by 12 to get the monthly rate. Then discount the future value using (1 + monthly rate) raised to the number of months. For example, a 9% annual rate becomes 0.75% monthly, and $5,000 in 36 months would be worth $3,820.74 today.
exam_tips: Always adjust the annual rate to the compounding period (e.g., divide by 12 for monthly).
Use the formula PV = FV / (1 + r/n)^(n*t) where n = compounding periods per year.

question.stem: What is the present value of $5,000 to be received in three years if the annual interest rate is 9% compounded monthly?

choices.why: Correct calculation using monthly compounding.
Common rounding error during manual computations.
Incorrectly assumes simple interest instead of compounding.

pills.title: Compounding Index Returns
pills.concept: To calculate an index’s total return over multiple periods, you compound the subperiod returns using a geometric formula. This accounts for the compounding effect of returns, unlike simple averages. For example, a 2% return followed by a 3% return results in a total return of (1.02 × 1.03) − 1 = 5.06%.
exam_tips: Always use geometric linking for total returns, not arithmetic averages.
Remember to subtract 1 after multiplying the factors.

question.stem: What is the total return for an index with subperiod returns of 2% and 3%?

choices.why: Arithmetic average of 2% and 3%, but correct method uses geometric linking.
Correct calculation: (1.02 × 1.03) − 1 = 5.06%.
Incorrectly assumes returns multiply directly (2% + 3% = 5%, but compounding requires (1.02 × 1.03) − 1).

pills.title: Collateralized Debt Obligations (CDOs)
pills.concept: CDOs are structured financial products that pool various debt instruments, like corporate bonds or loans, and issue tranches with different risk levels. Senior tranches have lower risk and lower returns, while subordinated tranches bear higher risk and higher potential returns. CDOs help investors diversify credit risk across multiple asset classes.
exam_tips: Remember that CDOs can be backed by different asset types (e.g., MBS, ABS, or loans).
Distinguish between synthetic CDOs (using credit default swaps) and cash CDOs (using actual debt instruments).

question.stem: Which of the following best describes a key feature of collateralized debt obligations (CDOs)?

choices.text: They are backed by a diversified pool of debt securities, with tranches offering varying risk/return profiles.
choices.why: This is correct. CDOs use tranching to segment risk, with senior and subordinated tranches.

choices.text: They are exclusively backed by residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS).
choices.why: This is incorrect. CDOs can be backed by various debt types, not just RMBS.

choices.text: They eliminate credit risk entirely for investors.
choices.why: This is incorrect. CDOs redistribute credit risk, but do not eliminate it.

pills.title: Calculating Income Tax Expense with Deferred Taxes
pills.concept: Income tax expense on the income statement includes both taxes owed now (taxes payable) and changes in deferred tax liabilities (DTL) and assets (DTA). The formula is: income tax expense = taxes payable + increase in DTL - increase in DTA. This reflects temporary differences between accounting and tax rules.
exam_tips: Always start with taxes payable when calculating income tax expense
Remember that increases in DTL add to expense while increases in DTA reduce it
pills.formula: Income tax expense = Taxes payable + ΔDTL − ΔDTA

question.stem: A company reports $150,000 in taxes payable for the year. Its deferred tax liabilities increased by $30,000 and deferred tax assets increased by $20,000. What is the income tax expense for the year?

choices.why: Correctly applies the formula: 150,000 + 30,000 - 20,000
Incorrectly subtracts DTL instead of adding it
Forgets to include changes in deferred taxes entirely

pills.title: Moneyness of Options
pills.concept: An option's moneyness describes whether it is in the money, out of the money, or at the money. For a call, it is in the money if the underlying price exceeds the strike price; for a put, it is in the money if the strike price exceeds the underlying price. At-the-money occurs when the underlying price equals the strike price.
exam_tips: Remember that intrinsic value is the profit from immediate exercise (if positive) or zero.
Time value reflects the potential for future price changes and always adds to intrinsic value before expiration.
pills.formula: Option premium = Intrinsic value + Time value

question.stem: A July 50 put option is trading on a stock currently priced at $47/share. What is the moneyness of this option?

choices.text: In the money
choices.why: Correct: The strike price ($50) is above the stock price ($47), so the put has intrinsic value.

choices.text: Out of the money
choices.why: Incorrect: This would be true if the stock price were above the strike price for a call or below for a put.

choices.text: At the money
choices.why: Incorrect: At-the-money occurs when the stock price equals the strike price.

pills.title: Elliott Wave Theory Basics
pills.concept: Elliott wave theory suggests that market prices move in predictable patterns: five waves in the direction of a trend (three of which are strong, two corrective) and three waves against the trend. Analysts often use Fibonacci ratios (like 0.618 or 1.618) to estimate price targets and identify support/resistance levels.
exam_tips: Focus on the 5-3 wave structure; avoid confusing it with other cyclical theories like Kondratieff waves.
Remember Fibonacci ratios are used for price projections, not for calculating returns.

question.stem: Which statement best describes Elliott wave theory?

choices.text: Prices follow a five-wave trend followed by a three-wave countertrend, with Fibonacci ratios used for analysis.
choices.why: This is the correct answer. It accurately describes the core of Elliott wave theory.

choices.text: It uses a 54-year cycle linked to economic expansions and contractions.
choices.why: This refers to Kondratieff waves, not Elliott wave theory. The cycles are unrelated.

choices.text: It relies on fundamental analysis of company financials and macroeconomic data.
choices.why: Elliott wave theory is purely technical, focusing on price patterns, not fundamentals.

pills.title: Commodity Exposure via Derivatives
pills.concept: Investors often use derivative securities like futures to gain exposure to commodity prices, as they offer cost-effective leverage and direct price tracking. Physical commodities or stocks of commodity-producing companies are less common choices due to higher costs, storage issues, and additional risks from company-specific factors.
exam_tips: Remember that derivatives are the most frequently used tool for commodity exposure in portfolios.
Differentiate between direct ownership (physical commodities) and indirect exposure (derivatives or equity).

question.stem: An investor seeking efficient exposure to oil price movements is most likely to choose:

choices.text: A portfolio of crude oil futures contracts
choices.why: Derivatives provide direct, leveraged exposure without physical storage costs.

choices.text: A diversified portfolio of oil-producing company stocks
choices.why: Stocks include company-specific risks and may not track oil prices precisely.

choices.text: Physical barrels of crude oil stored in warehouses
choices.why: Physical ownership involves high storage costs and logistical challenges.

pills.title: Evaluating Stock Mispricing with CAPM/SML
pills.concept: The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) calculates a stock’s required return using its beta and market risk premium. If a stock’s expected return is lower than its required return (based on CAPM), it is overvalued and plots below the Security Market Line (SML). If expected return is higher, it is undervalued and plots above the SML. Properly valued stocks lie exactly on the SML.
exam_tips: Remember: Overvalued stocks have expected returns below the SML; undervalued stocks are above it.
Always compute required return using CAPM before comparing to expected return.

question.stem: A stock has an expected return of 10%, a beta of 0.8, a risk-free rate of 3%, and a market return of 12%. Is the stock overvalued, undervalued, or properly valued?

choices.text: Overvalued
choices.why: The required return (3% + 0.8×(12%−3%) = 11.4%) is higher than the expected return (10%), so the stock plots below the SML.

choices.text: Undervalued
choices.why: This would be true if the expected return exceeded the required return, but the calculation here shows the opposite.

choices.text: Properly valued
choices.why: This would require the expected return to equal the required return (11.4%), which it does not.

pills.title: Present Value of a Single Sum
pills.concept: The present value (PV) calculates today's value of a future cash flow, considering a discount rate. It answers: How much money do I need today to reach a specific future amount? For example, $200 in two years at 10% equals about $165.29 today.
exam_tips: Always check if cash flows are at the beginning or end of periods (annuities due vs. ordinary annuities).
Use the formula PV = FV / (1 + I/Y)^N for single sums.

question.stem: What is the present value of $500 received in 5 years at a 12% discount rate?

choices.why: Correct calculation using PV formula
Forgets to discount the future value
Confuses present value with future value

pills.title: Confidence Intervals and Hypothesis Tests Relationship
pills.concept: A confidence interval and a hypothesis test are connected through critical values. For a two-tailed test at a 5% significance level, the 95% confidence interval uses the same critical value (±1.96) as the hypothesis test. If a hypothesized value (e.g., 0) lies outside the confidence interval, the null hypothesis is rejected, similar to when a test statistic exceeds the critical value.
exam_tips: Remember that confidence intervals and hypothesis tests share critical values tied to the significance level.
Reject the null hypothesis if the hypothesized value is outside the confidence interval.
pills.formula: Confidence Interval = Sample Statistic ± (Critical Value × Standard Error)

question.stem: A 95% confidence interval for a population mean is [0.069%, 0.131%]. If the null hypothesis states the mean is 0, what is the correct conclusion?

choices.text: Reject the null hypothesis because 0 is not in the interval.
choices.why: Correct. The hypothesized value (0) lies outside the confidence interval, leading to rejection of the null.

choices.text: Fail to reject the null hypothesis because the interval is narrow.
choices.why: Incorrect. The width of the interval does not determine the decision; inclusion of the hypothesized value does.

choices.text: Accept the null hypothesis because the interval includes 0.1%.
choices.why: Incorrect. The null hypothesis is about the hypothesized value (0), not the sample mean (0.1%).

pills.title: Fiscal Policy Tools and Objectives
pills.concept: Fiscal policy uses government spending and taxation to influence the economy. Its goals include boosting economic activity, redistributing income, and guiding resource allocation. Tools include spending (e.g., transfer payments, infrastructure projects) and revenue (e.g., taxes). Unlike monetary policy, fiscal policy directly impacts public spending and taxation levels.
exam_tips: Distinguish fiscal policy tools (spending/revenue) from monetary policy (interest rates, money supply)
Remember time lags in fiscal policy implementation (recognition, action, impact)

question.stem: Which of the following is a primary objective of fiscal policy?

choices.text: Influencing economic activity and redistributing income
choices.why: Correct: LOS 16.o explicitly states these as key objectives

choices.text: Maintaining price stability through interest rate adjustments
choices.why: Incorrect: Price stability is a monetary policy goal, not fiscal

choices.text: Balancing the government budget annually
choices.why: Incorrect: Budget balance is an outcome, not a primary objective

pills.title: Deferred Tax Liability Calculation
pills.concept: A deferred tax liability (DTL) arises when the tax base of an asset is less than its carrying value on the balance sheet. This creates a future obligation to pay more taxes, often due to temporary differences like accelerated depreciation for tax purposes. For example, if an asset’s carrying value is $400,000 and its tax base is $300,000, the $100,000 difference multiplied by the tax rate (e.g., 40%) results in a $40,000 DTL.
exam_tips: Identify temporary differences between financial and tax accounting (e.g., depreciation methods).
Use the current statutory tax rate, not historical rates, when calculating DTLs.
pills.formula: Deferred Tax Liability = (Carrying Value - Tax Base) × Tax Rate

question.stem: Which formula correctly calculates a deferred tax liability (DTL)?

choices.text: (Carrying Value - Tax Base) × Tax Rate
choices.why: This is the correct formula for DTL when carrying value exceeds tax base.

choices.text: (Tax Base - Carrying Value) × Tax Rate
choices.why: This calculates a deferred tax asset (DTA), not a DTL.

choices.text: (Carrying Value + Tax Base) × Tax Rate
choices.why: This adds values instead of comparing them, which is irrelevant to DTL calculations.

pills.title: Holding Period Return and Investment Horizon
pills.concept: When a bond is held to maturity, its holding period return equals the YTM at purchase, assuming reinvestment rates match the YTM. However, if the investment horizon is shorter than the bond’s maturity and interest rates change, the return differs. A rise in YTM before the sale date reduces the bond’s price, lowering the return, while a drop in YTM increases the price, raising the return.
exam_tips: Remember that market price risk (price volatility) and reinvestment risk (uncertainty in coupon reinvestment) trade off depending on the investment horizon.
Shorter holding periods amplify market price risk’s impact on returns compared to reinvestment risk.
pills.formula: Holding Period Return = (Ending Value + Income - Initial Investment) / Initial Investment

question.stem: An investor buys a 3-year 6% annual coupon bond at par ($1,000) with a 1-year investment horizon. If YTM rises to 7% immediately after purchase, what is the investor’s holding period return?

choices.why: Correct. The bond price drops to $981.92 after 1 year, and the return is (981.92 + 60 - 1,000)/1,000 = 4.19%.
Tempting if assuming YTM equals return, but ignores price changes due to YTM shifts.
Confuses reinvestment rate with holding period return; YTM affects price, not directly the return.

pills.title: Factors Affecting Option Value: Underlying Asset Price
pills.concept: The price of the underlying asset directly impacts option values. For call options, higher underlying prices increase intrinsic value, making the option more valuable. For put options, the opposite occurs: higher underlying prices reduce their value. This is because call options benefit from rising asset prices, while put options profit from declines.
exam_tips: Remember that call and put options move in opposite directions relative to the underlying price.
Link this concept to intrinsic value calculations during exams.
pills.formula: Option premium = intrinsic value + time value

question.stem: How does an increase in the underlying asset price affect a call option and a put option?

choices.text: Call option value increases; put option value decreases
choices.why: Correct. Call options benefit from higher asset prices, while put options lose value as the underlying rises.

choices.text: Both call and put option values increase
choices.why: Incorrect. Put options decrease in value when the underlying price rises.

choices.text: Both call and put option values decrease
choices.why: Incorrect. Call options increase in value with higher underlying prices.

pills.title: PV of a Bond's Cash Flows
pills.concept: A bond's value is the sum of the present value of its coupon payments (treated as an ordinary annuity) and the present value of its face value (a lump sum). For example, a 6-year bond with annual coupons requires calculating the PV of six $70 payments plus the PV of the $1,000 face value.
exam_tips: Always separate coupon payments (annuity) and face value (lump sum) for accurate PV calculations.
Ensure calculator inputs match the cash flow timing (end-of-period for ordinary annuities).
pills.formula: PV = PV(annuity) + PV(lump sum) = PMT × [1 - (1 + r)^-n]/r + FV/(1 + r)^n

question.stem: What is the present value of a 5-year bond with a 6% annual coupon and a $1,000 face value, assuming a 7% discount rate?

choices.why: Correct. Uses annuity formula for coupons and lump sum for face value.
Incorrect. Assumes discount rate equals coupon rate, which is only true at par.
Incorrect. Likely miscalculated annuity factor or discounted face value incorrectly.

pills.title: Adjusting Price-Weighted Indexes for Stock Splits
pills.concept: When a stock in a price-weighted index splits, the index’s denominator is adjusted to maintain its value. For example, if Stock C splits 2-for-1, its price is halved, but the index denominator changes to offset this, ensuring the index value remains stable unless other stock prices change.
exam_tips: Focus on how splits affect individual stock prices and the index’s denominator, not the numerator.
Remember that price-weighted indexes are sensitive to high-priced stocks, which can distort returns.
pills.formula: (Sum of adjusted stock prices) / new denominator = original index value

question.stem: A price-weighted index includes Stock A ($10), Stock B ($20), and Stock C ($90). After Stock C splits 2-for-1, what is the new denominator if the index value must stay at 40?

choices.why: Mistakenly uses original denominator without adjusting for the split.
Incorrectly averages the split-adjusted price with original prices.

pills.title: Deferred Tax Liability with Changing Tax Rates
pills.concept: A deferred tax liability (DTL) is calculated by multiplying the expected tax rate for the year by the difference between an asset’s carrying value (financial reporting) and its tax base (tax reporting). If the tax rate changes, the new rate applies to the temporary difference in that year, not the original rate.
exam_tips: Always use the tax rate expected in the year of the temporary difference, not the original rate.
Verify the temporary difference by subtracting tax base from carrying value, not the other way around.
pills.formula: DTL = Expected Tax Rate × (Carrying Value − Tax Base)

question.stem: A firm has a temporary difference of $10,000 in Year 2. The tax rate was 30% in Year 1 but decreases to 25% in Year 2. What is the Year 2 deferred tax liability?

choices.why: Correct: Uses the Year 2 tax rate (25%) × $10,000 temporary difference.
Incorrect: Applies the Year 1 tax rate (30%) instead of the updated rate.
Incorrect: Misapplies the temporary difference (e.g., $10,000 × 25% = $2,500, not $1,000).

pills.title: Choosing Between t-Test and z-Test
pills.concept: When testing a population mean, choose a z-test if the population variance is known or the sample is large (n ≥ 30) with unknown variance. Use a t-test only when the population variance is unknown and the sample is small (n < 30), provided the population is normal or approximately normal. The t-test adjusts for uncertainty in the sample standard deviation, while the z-test relies on known population parameters or large-sample approximations.
exam_tips: Memorize the rule: t-test for small samples with unknown variance, z-test for large samples or known variance.
Be cautious: Even with unknown variance, large samples allow z-tests due to the Central Limit Theorem.

question.stem: A researcher tests a sample of 300 observations with unknown population variance. Which test statistic should be used?

choices.text: z-test
choices.why: Correct: Large samples allow z-tests even with unknown variance because the t and z distributions converge.

choices.text: t-test
choices.why: Incorrect: The t-test is only required for small samples with unknown variance.

choices.text: Either test is equally valid
choices.why: Incorrect: While both tests might give similar results, the z-test is preferred for large samples due to computational simplicity.

pills.title: Comparative Advantage and Opportunity Cost
pills.concept: A country has a comparative advantage in producing a good if it can do so at a lower opportunity cost than another country. Opportunity cost is what you give up to produce one more unit of a good. Even if a country is less productive overall (absolute disadvantage), it can still benefit from trade by specializing in goods where its opportunity cost is lowest.
exam_tips: Focus on opportunity costs, not absolute production levels when determining comparative advantage.
Calculate opportunity costs using the formula: (Quantity of Good A given up) / (Quantity of Good B produced).
pills.formula: Opportunity Cost = (Quantity of Good A given up) / (Quantity of Good B produced)

question.stem: Country X can produce 100 units of wine or 50 units of cheese. Country Y can produce 60 units of wine or 30 units of cheese. Which country has a comparative advantage in wine production?

choices.text: Country Y
choices.why: Confuses absolute advantage with comparative advantage; both countries have the same opportunity cost for wine (0.5 cheese per unit of wine).

choices.text: Neither country
choices.why: Incorrectly assumes identical opportunity costs when calculations show they are equal.

choices.text: Both countries equally
choices.why: Correct. Both have the same opportunity cost (0.5 cheese per wine), so neither has a comparative advantage in wine.

pills.title: Present Value of a Perpetuity
pills.concept: A perpetuity is a series of equal payments made forever. Its present value equals the annual payment divided by the discount rate. For example, if a perpetuity pays $5 annually at a 10% discount rate, its PV is $50 ($5 / 0.10). This formula assumes the first payment occurs one period from now.
exam_tips: Remember the formula: PV = PMT / I/Y. This is a common calculation in corporate finance and valuation.
Watch out for deferred perpetuities, where the first payment is delayed. These require discounting the perpetuity’s value back to the present.

question.stem: What is the present value of a perpetuity that pays $4.50 annually forever, with a required rate of return of 8%?

choices.why: Incorrect. This would be the case if the discount rate were 10% (4.50 / 0.10).
Incorrect. This answer incorrectly divides the payment by 0.01 instead of 0.08.

pills.title: Effective Duration for Bonds with Embedded Options
pills.concept: Effective duration measures interest rate sensitivity for bonds with embedded options (e.g., callable or prepayable bonds) because their cash flows depend on future interest rate paths. Unlike modified duration, which assumes fixed cash flows, effective duration uses a benchmark yield curve to account for uncertainty in cash flows caused by options like prepayments or calls.
exam_tips: Remember: Effective duration is required for bonds with options, while modified duration applies to option-free bonds.
Link effective duration to the need for a pricing model that reflects changing interest rate environments.

question.stem: Effective duration is preferred over modified duration for bonds with embedded options because these bonds:

choices.text: Have higher convexity than option-free bonds.
choices.why: Convexity relates to the curvature of price-yield relationships, not the reason for using effective duration.

choices.text: Are more sensitive to credit risk changes.
choices.why: Credit risk is addressed separately via credit duration, not effective duration.

choices.text: Have cash flows that depend on future interest rate paths.
choices.why: Correct. Embedded options (e.g., calls, prepayments) create cash flow uncertainty tied to interest rate movements, which effective duration explicitly accounts for.

pills.title: Put-Call Parity for European Options
pills.concept: Put-call parity shows the relationship between European call and put options with the same strike price and expiration. It ensures no-arbitrage opportunities by linking the call price, put price, stock price, and present value of the strike price. The formula reflects that a combination of a call and a bond equals a combination of a put and the underlying stock.
exam_tips: Memorize the formula and understand its components: call price (c), put price (p), stock price (S), strike price (X), and risk-free rate (Rf).
Note that put-call parity applies only to European options, not American ones, and assumes no dividends or transaction costs.

question.stem: According to put-call parity for European options, which of the following equations is correct?

choices.text: c + X / (1 + Rf)^T = S + p
choices.why: This is the correct put-call parity formula for European options with no dividends.

choices.text: c - X / (1 + Rf)^T = S - p
choices.why: This incorrectly subtracts the present value of the strike price and misrepresents the relationship.

choices.text: c + X / (1 + Rf)^T = S - p
